items.note: What staff say the moment you walk in ANYWHERE in Japan — shops, restaurants, convenience stores, hotels. It is a RITUAL greeting, not a personal one. You do NOT need to reply. A small nod or nothing at all is perfectly fine. Replying 「ありがとう」is a little odd. If you feel awkward just smiling and nodding is 100% correct. The word comes from いらっしゃる (to be/come/go — honorific). Staff may say it continuously as you browse; still no response needed.

english: How many people? (restaurant staff asking your party size)
items.note: 名様 (mei-sama) = number of people (ultra-polite: 様 is the ultimate honorific). They're asking how many seats you need. Reply: 「一人です」(one), 「二人です」(two), 「三人です」(three). Or hold up fingers — totally acceptable. If you have a reservation: 「予約しています。〜です」= I have a reservation. My name is ~. Staff will then say 「こちらへどうぞ」(right this way) or 「少々お待ちください」(please wait a moment).

english: Right this way, please (leading you to your seat)
items.note: こちら = this direction (polite). どうぞ = please / go ahead. Said while gesturing for you to follow. Just follow! You can say 「ありがとうございます」or nothing. If you prefer a different seat: 「窓側の席はありますか？」= Do you have a window seat? 「カウンター席でもいいですか？」= Is a counter seat okay? (counter seats are great for solo dining and watching the chef).

items.kana: しょうしょうおまちください
english: Please wait a moment (staff asking you to wait)
items.note: 少々 (shoushou) = a little while (very polite). お待ちください = please wait (お + 待つ + ください). The humble/polite version of 「ちょっと待ってください」. You might hear this when a table isn't ready, they need to check something, or they're preparing your order. Just reply 「はい、わかりました」(yes, understood) or nod. If you've been waiting a long time: 「まだですか？」= Is it not ready yet?

english: Let's eat / I humbly receive (ritual phrase before eating)
items.note: One of the most important cultural phrases. Said BEFORE eating, hands together or with a bow. いただく = to humbly receive (humble form of もらう). You're expressing gratitude for the food, the person who cooked it, the animals that gave their lives, and nature itself. It has no single English equivalent. NOT a prayer — it's cultural. Skipping it is fine as a foreigner, but saying it will genuinely delight any Japanese host. The ending: 「ごちそうさまでした」.

english: Thank you for the meal (said after eating, leaving a restaurant)
items.note: Said when you finish eating OR when leaving a restaurant. ご馳走 (gochisou) = a feast/treat. 様 (sama) = honorific. でした = past tense. Literally 'you went to great lengths to provide this feast'. How to use: (1) Put down chopsticks and say it quietly. (2) Say it loudly to the kitchen/chef as you leave — they genuinely love hearing this. It's also said when someone treats you to a meal: 「ごちそうさまでした！」= Thank you for dinner! Short form: 「ごちそうさま！」.

items.script: お会計をお願いします
items.kana: おかいけいをおねがいします
items.note: お会計 (okaikei) = the bill/check (polite). How to ask: catch a staff member's eye and say this, OR mime a writing gesture (drawing a square in the air = the universal Japan sign for 'bill please'). Also fine: 「すみません、お会計！」(excuse me, bill!). In Japan you usually PAY AT THE REGISTER (レジ), not at the table — staff will hand you a slip, you take it to the counter. 「レジでお支払いください」= Please pay at the register.

items.kana: かーどはつかえますか
items.note: カード = card. 使えます (tsukaemasu) = can use (potential of 使う). Japan is improving but still cash-heavy. If the answer is no: 「現金のみです」= Cash only. In that case, nearby ATMs: コンビニ ATMs (7-Eleven, FamilyMart, Lawson) accept foreign cards reliably. 「近くにATMはありますか？」= Is there an ATM nearby? Also ask: 「クレジットカードは大丈夫ですか？」= Is a credit card ok? Visa and Mastercard now work almost everywhere; Amex less so.

What staff say if cards aren't accepted. 現金 (genkin) = cash. のみ = only. You'll hear this at small local restaurants, street food stalls, some shrines. Plan ahead: always carry ¥5,000–¥10,000 in cash. The response: 「わかりました」= understood. 「近くにATMはありますか？」= Is there an ATM nearby? Some shops now also accept: PayPay (smartphone QR payment), Suica (transport card — top up in cash). 「Suicaは使えますか？」= Can I use Suica?

items.kana: ふくろはよろしいですか
english: Do you need a bag? (shop staff asking)
items.note: よろしいですか = is it alright? (polite form of いいですか). Staff ask this because Japan now charges for plastic bags (¥2-5). Responses: 「大丈夫です」= No need, I'm fine. OR 「いただけますか？ / お願いします」= Yes please. If you bring your own: 「袋は持ってきました」= I brought my own bag. The same phrase structure works for other staff questions: 「温めはよろしいですか？」= Shall I heat it up? 「お箸はよろしいですか？」= Do you need chopsticks?

items.script: ポイントカードはお持ちですか
items.kana: ぽいんとかーどはおもちですか
romaji: pointo kaado wa omochi desu ka
english: Do you have a points card? (shop staff asking)
items.note: ポイントカード = loyalty/points card. お持ちですか = do you have? (polite: お + 持つ). You will be asked this at almost every Japanese shop. As a tourist, you don't have one. Responses: 「持っていません」= I don't have one. 「結構です」= No thank you / I'm fine. 「いいえ」alone can sound abrupt. Getting a points card: some shops like Bic Camera, Yodobashi, or drug stores give one on the spot and the discount is real — worth it for bigger purchases.

english: Shall I heat it up? (convenience store asking about food)
items.note: The question every convenience store staff asks when you buy prepared food. 温める (atatameru) = to heat/warm. Response: 「はい、お願いします！」= Yes please! OR 「大丈夫です」= No, it's fine. If you buy a rice ball and an instant noodle cup, they may ask separately about each. If you're unsure whether something should be heated: 「これは温めた方がいいですか？」= Is it better to heat this one? They know their products and will advise you.

items.script: お箸でよろしいですか
items.kana: おはしでよろしいですか
english: Are chopsticks alright? / Will chopsticks be ok?
items.note: お箸 (ohashi) = chopsticks (polite: お prefix). Staff ask this to check if you need a fork instead. As someone learning Japanese, saying yes is great practice! If you need a fork: 「フォークをいただけますか？」= Could I have a fork please? 「スプーンをいただけますか？」= Could I have a spoon? Chopstick etiquette: don't stick them upright in rice (funeral image), don't pass food chopstick-to-chopstick (also funeral), and do say 「いただきます」before eating.

乾 (kan) = dry/drain. 杯 (pai) = cup/glass. 乾杯 = 'drain the cup' = Cheers! This is THE toast phrase in Japan. Wait for the host to initiate 乾杯 before drinking — jumping in early is rude. Raise your glass slightly lower than the most senior person's glass (seniority hierarchy). The first drink after 乾杯 is usually beer (生ビール, nama biiru = draft beer) or a non-alcoholic option: 「ソフトドリンクでもいいですか？」= Is a soft drink okay? Saying 乾杯 with genuine enthusiasm wins hearts.

english: Please come again / See you next time (staff farewell)
items.note: また = again. どうぞ = please. Said by staff as you leave a restaurant or shop. You don't need to reply, but if you want to be warm: 「ありがとうございました！」or 「ごちそうさまでした！」(in a restaurant). If you genuinely want to come back: 「また来ます！」= I'll come again! This phrase is often heard alongside: 「ありがとうございました！」(thank you very much) and 「またのお越しをお待ちしております」(we look forward to your next visit — very formal, hotels/dept stores).

english: Sorry to keep you waiting (staff to you, after a wait)
items.note: お待たせ = making you wait (humble). しました = did. Said by staff when they return after a delay, bring your food, or finish a task. You don't need to reply. If you feel like being friendly: 「いいえ、大丈夫です」= No, it's fine. Also useful YOU → someone else: 「お待たせしました！」if you made someone wait for you. Variation: 「長らくお待たせいたしました」= I'm very sorry to have kept you waiting so long (very formal apology for a long wait).

items.script: よろしくお願いします
items.kana: よろしくおねがいします
english: Please / I leave it to you / Nice to meet you / Thank you (incredibly versatile)
items.note: The single most versatile phrase in Japanese. よろしく = 'in a good/suitable way'. お願いします = please (I request). Contexts: (1) Meeting someone: 「どうぞよろしくお願いします」= Nice to meet you / I'm in your care. (2) Asking someone to handle something: 「ではよろしくお願いします」= Please take care of it / I'm counting on you. (3) Ordering at a restaurant: 「〜をひとつ、よろしくお願いします」= One ~, if you please. (4) Leaving for the day: 「よろしくお願いします」= I leave things in your hands. Saying it too much is impossible.

english: Excuse me! (calling staff, getting attention, apologising)
items.note: The single most useful word in Japan. Three core uses: (1) CALLING STAFF: say it clearly to flag down a waiter — 「すみません！」while raising your hand slightly. This is the correct and common way to call staff. (2) EXCUSE ME: squeezing past people, bumping into someone. (3) LIGHT APOLOGY: 'I'm sorry to trouble you'. Heavier apology = 「申し訳ありません」(moushiwake arimasen) or 「ごめんなさい」(gomen nasai). At a konbini register, 「すみません」is fine for any small request: 「すみません、袋をください」.
